item.title: Rise in HIV infection a worry
item.description: A SENIOR health worker from the Catholic Church Health Services (CCHS) has raised serious concerns about rising human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection rates among young people in Morobe. Lae CCHS health manager Sister Knowledge Ndlovu told delegates at a review meeting of the 3,979 walk-in patients tested for HIV in 2025, 184 returned positive results, with the majority of new infections con
item.content:encoded:
A SENIOR health worker from the Catholic Church Health Services (CCHS) has raised serious concerns about rising human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection rates among young people in Morobe.

Lae CCHS health manager Sister Knowledge Ndlovu told delegates at a review meeting of the 3,979 walk-in patients tested for HIV in 2025, 184 returned positive results, with the majority of new infections concentrated among youth between the ages of 15 to 30.

“The most alarming thing is that those who are positive were actually between the ages of 15 to 30 and that’s the highest percentage,” Knowledge stated.

The figures from the first quarter of 2026, which showed that 33 patients had already tested positive out of 1,088 tested.

“You have 33 cases in three months,” she said.

“Importantly, these figures do not represent Catholics alone.

“CCHS facilities are open to the general public.”

CCHS operates a network of facilities across the province, including the Mercy health centre, Mary Queen of Peace health sub-centre, the 11-Mile Bishop Henry Community health post, St Francis aid post in Wau and St Dominic aid post in Wauwoka.

CCHS currently manages 1,369 HIV patients on its database, with 848 actively on treatment, of which 368 are male and 569 female.

The data signals an urgent need for targeted HIV prevention and education programmes directed at young people in Morobe, particularly as infection rates among the 15-30 age group continue to climb.

Alongside its clinical reporting, CCHS had launched a comprehensive outreach programme, with a series of health visits scheduled across the province throughout this year.

The outreaches began in February with a visit to Cold Mountain, Watut, followed by Tararan on March 19 and Bau at Morobe Patrol Post from March 16-20.

In April, the team reached Erap Boys Town on the 29, while May’s outreach was planned for Gumi, Watut, this week   from May 25-29.

Next month, the team would go to Labu from June 23-25, and onto Watarais from July 28-30.

The second half of the year continues with Bukawa in August, Gobari on Sept 23, and Yalu on Oct 14.

The final outreach of the year is set for Wauwoka, Menyamya, from Nov 16-20.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Students donate blood after reported shortage
item.description: THIRTEEN Divine Word University students recently donated blood at Madang’s Modilon General Hospital after its blood bank reported a shortage. Theresha Orere, a final-year Bachelor of Environmental Health programme student said: “We received word that the blood bank was experiencing a shortage of blood supplies which inspired us to organise this initiative.”
item.content:encoded:
THIRTEEN Divine Word University students recently donated blood at Madang’s Modilon General Hospital after its blood bank reported a shortage.

Theresha Orere, a final-year Bachelor of Environmental Health programme student said: “We received word that the blood bank was experiencing a shortage of blood supplies which inspired us to organise this initiative.”

Previous student-led activities and community programmes often focused on donating toiletries and other basic necessities.

So they wanted to contribute in a different and more impactful way.

“One that could directly help save lives,” Orere said.

The students are part of a youth foundation involved in community service and empowerment.

“This initiative is our way of giving back to the Madang community for embracing us, supporting our education journey and making Madang our home over the years,” Orere said.

Since the establishment of Ohana Foundation in 2024, they have carried out awareness programmes and community-based initiatives aimed at making a positive impact.

Orere said one of the major challenges they faced was encouraging people to donate blood because of misconceptions surrounding the process.

“Another challenge was donor eligibility as many participants were unable to donate because of low hemoglobin levels,” she said.

“This highlighted the importance of maintaining healthy eating habits and proper nutrition.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Maru orders probe into foreign firms
item.description: INTERNATIONAL Trade and Investment Minister Richard Maru says an investigation will be conducted into certain foreign-owned companies operating in West New Britain whose operations have been affecting local businesses. Maru said officers from his department and other authorities would be sent to the province to investigate the concerns raised by Talasea MP Freddie Kumai in Parliament yesterday.
item.content:encoded:
INTERNATIONAL Trade and Investment Minister Richard Maru says an investigation will be conducted into certain foreign-owned companies operating in West New Britain whose operations have been affecting local businesses.

Maru said officers from his department and other authorities would be sent to the province to investigate the concerns raised by Talasea MP Freddie Kumai in Parliament yesterday.

Kumai had called on the Government to investigate foreign companies engaging in business activities that should be reserved for locals.

“They are dominating construction and civil works, oil palm replanting and excavation contracts, creating a de facto monopoly and marginalising local businesses,” he said.

“Local benefits are being extracted out of the province and the country, rather than circulating in local communities.

“Their rights to participate in business in their own country, as envisaged under the Constitution and national development policies, are being undermined.

“There are also serious questions as to whether these companies are registered as foreign or PNG companies, complying with all PNG laws and regulatory requirements, paying the correct taxes, lodging company and annual returns, and only employing foreign workers with valid work permits in accordance with our labour and immigration laws.”

Maru said he was not aware of the foreign companies in question but said that the Government would address the concerns raised.

“We are going to deal with it,” he said.

“Bring the complaint to me in writing so I can take a look at it and talk to my other colleague ministers and we can send in a government team to go and investigate the allegations.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Promote our tourist sites: Tour guide
item.description: MANY remote tourism sites with fascinating cultural attractions for tourists have not been adequately promoted and made accessible, according to a local tour guide from Angoram, East Sepik. Jeffery Mann, from Kanigara village in the Black Water area of Angoram, said the Tourism Promotion Authority (TPA) needed to do more to promote and expose rare cultural sites, practices and norms to the outside
item.content:encoded:
MANY remote tourism sites with fascinating cultural attractions for tourists have not been adequately promoted and made accessible, according to a local tour guide from Angoram, East Sepik.

Jeffery Mann, from Kanigara village in the Black Water area of Angoram, said the Tourism Promotion Authority (TPA) needed to do more to promote and expose rare cultural sites, practices and norms to the outside world.

Mann, who is also a village magistrate, is team leader of the Kanigara Village Sepik Haus Tambaran (Spirit Haus) that performs “crocodile pattern skin cutting” a traditional ritual practised as an initiation rite inside the haus tambaran for young boys growing into adulthood.

Mann said the Government, through the Culture and Tourism Ministry and TPA, needed to revisit many tourism sites in the country to see the potential of these areas.

“In Angoram we have six villages that practice crocodile-skin cutting; they are Kanigara, Gavamas, Karabit, Tambraman, Tumgibit and Songreman,” he said.

Mann made the remarks when thanking Eastern Highlands-based Caspar Guides Services, for bringing 15 European tourists to Kanigara village at the beginning of this month.

He thanked Caspar Guides Services for giving them opportunities to earn money.

Mann called on Caspar Guides Services to engage other villages in Angoram and other parts of East Sepik as they had similar traditions and customary practices which tourists would find interesting.

He also acknowledged two of the tourists, Miguel Angel and Gonzalez Buch, who participated in the sacred initiation services inside the haus tambaran .

Caspar Guide Services general manager Caspar Dama said despite the financial hardships his operation remained committed to delivering tourism products.

He thanked Mann and Kanigara villagers for showcasing their unique traditional practice of crocodile skin cutting.

Dama said he would continue to bring tourists to Angoram and other parts of Sepik and the country that could offer tourists unique cultural experiences in the form of traditional rituals and festivals as well as nature and wildlife.

He said the same group of Tourists from Spain were also taken to Simbai area in the upper Ramu in Madang.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: NEST scores big on Australia funds
item.description: The NEST Club of Tokarara in Port Moresby is celebrating a milestone achievement after being named one of the successful recipients of the Australian government’s inaugural Pilai Long Stap Wantaim (play for unity) Fund. The fund, a small grants initiative, is designed to strengthen community development and youth empowerment in Papua New Guinea through sports.
item.content:encoded:
The NEST Club of Tokarara in Port Moresby is celebrating a milestone achievement after being named one of the successful recipients of the Australian government’s inaugural Pilai Long Stap Wantaim (play for unity) Fund.

The fund, a small grants initiative, is designed to strengthen community development and youth empowerment in Papua New Guinea through sports.

Founded by former PNG sprint sensation Nelson Stone, the NEST Community Sports Programme was among 23 organisations selected to receive support under the programme.

Stone said the recognition is a major boost for grassroots organisations that continue to work tirelessly in communities despite limited resources.

“It means a lot because not many of these kinds of grants come along from corporate organisations or government departments,” Stone told The National .

“For the Australian government to come out with this programme and support us, it means a lot for all the recipients because we do a lot for our communities.”

The funding would help NEST continue its mission of guiding and supporting young people through sport.

Established more than 10 years ago in Tokarara, the programme uses rugby league and athletics as tools to inspire positive change.

Over the years, NEST has produced successful athletes and rugby league players who have gone on to higher levels of competition, including representation with the PNG Hunters.

“Our programme mainly focuses on rugby league and athletics,” Stone explained.

“Through these two sports, we’ve been able to drive positive initiatives in the community.”

He added that one of the proudest achievements of the programme is seeing young players progress into elite rugby league pathways.

“A lot of kids are now coming through the programme, and that’s the impact it has had in our community back in Tokarara,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: NSW still favoured. Opener in Sydney Blues’ to lose despite Moses loss
item.description: CANBERRA’S Ethan Strange will make his State of Origin debut at five-eighth for New South Wales on tonight against Queensland in the 2026 series opener at Accor Stadium after Mitchell Moses was ruled out due to a hamstring injury. Moses pulled up with hamstring tightness after training in Gosford on Monday with scans revealing a minor hamstring strain for the Eels star.
item.content:encoded:
CANBERRA’S Ethan Strange will make his State of Origin debut at five-eighth for New South Wales on tonight against Queensland in the 2026 series opener at Accor Stadium after Mitchell Moses was ruled out due to a hamstring injury.

Moses pulled up with hamstring tightness after training in Gosford on Monday with scans revealing a minor hamstring strain for the Eels star.

The NSW Blues confirmed yesterday that 21-year-old Strange would start at five-eighth in Game 1 and that call by coach Laurie Daley has not impacted greatly the odds with the home side still favoured to win by the bookmakers with NSW starting $1.53 and the Queensland Maroons at $2.50.

Bulldogs playmaker Matt Burton joined Blues camp on Monday night and will be added to Daley’s the six-man interchange bench.

The hamstring injury is a cruel blow for Moses, who played in the opening game of last year’s series before suffering a calf injury at training in the lead-up to Game Two.

In 2024 the Eels star missed Game One due to a foot injury before returning for Games Two and Three to help steer the Blues to a series victory.

Cameron Munster sat out Queensland training on Sunday, but the Maroons insist their captain and star playmaker is in no doubt for Wednesday night’s Origin opener at Accor Stadium.

Broncos five-eighth Ezra Mam ran in Munster’s place during Sunday’s session at Sunshine Coast Stadium, with Queensland officials saying the Storm star was being rested due to general soreness/tightness.

Munster did some passing with Queensland great Allan Langer, a member of the Maroons coaching staff, and also oversaw his team-mates doing some running drills.

Centre Robert Toia trained in a no-contact orange bib after suffering a category-two HIA at Magic Round for Sydney Roosters, but he is also not in doubt.

The Westpac NSW Blues are out to defend home turf as they look to topple Queensland in the opening game of the 2026 Ampol State of Origin series.

Blues coach Daley has named a new-look squad for the clash as he looks to bounce back from last year’s defeat.

A capacity crowd exceeding 80,000 is expected at Accor Stadium, with fans set to turn the stadium sky blue in support of their team.

Six NSW players are in the mix to make Origin debuts, with Addin Fonua-Blake and Tolu Koula named to start. Victor Radley, Ethan Strange, Blayke Brailey and Casey McLean have all been listed on the six-man bench.

Daley has made no secret of his desire for the Blues to take on an attacking mindset and has recalled former skipper James Tedesco at fullback.

While home ground advantage has always played a big role in the 46-year history of State of Origin, visiting teams have enjoyed some success in recent times.

The home team hasn’t won an Origin game since 2023, when NSW prevailed 24-10 at Accor Stadium.

The reigning champions have plenty of stability from last year but there have been a handful of changes to the side that won the decider at Accor Stadium.

An injury to Tom Dearden has opened the door for halfback Sam Walker to make his Origin debut, while Kalyn Ponga edged Reece Walsh in the race for the fullback jumper. – NRL.com

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Lae selection trials signal change
item.description: THE Lae Football Association (LFA) will hold selection trials this weekend for three national representative squads, marking a new era in preparation for sanctioned competitions. The trials will determine the senior men’s, senior women’s, and Under 18 boys teams, reflecting a shift towards structured, forward planning under LFA’s leadership.
item.content:encoded:
THE Lae Football Association (LFA) will hold selection trials this weekend for three national representative squads, marking a new era in preparation for sanctioned competitions.

The trials will determine the senior men’s, senior women’s, and Under 18 boys teams, reflecting a shift towards structured, forward planning under LFA’s leadership.

President Ronald Ram highlighted that the association is moving away from last minute arrangements.

“LFA is trying to do away with short term invitation notice and trying to do things in advance in preparation for the invitation,” Ram said.

These squads will represent Lae at all PNG Football Association sanctioned tournaments, strengthening the city’s presence on the national football stage.

Match commissioner Alex Toki welcomed the initiative, describing it as a reflection of the association’s fresh direction.

“This is a new approach that LFA is taking, and it is a better way of doing things,” Toki said.

Central to the trials’ success is cooperation from clubs across the association.

Ram urged clubs to assist referees filling out player start lists to support coaches during selection.

The approach signals a new era, with officials confident that earlier preparation will translate into stronger performances when Lae’s representative sides take the field.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Watson confirms talks to move to PNG Chiefs
item.description: CONNOR Watson has confirmed he is seriously considering a move to the PNG Chiefs, revealing that contract talks with the expansion side have been ongoing for several weeks. The Sydney Roosters utility also admitted he was impressed by what he saw during his recent visit to Port Moresby, saying the reality of Papua New Guinea was very different from the image often shown in the media.
item.content:encoded:
CONNOR Watson has confirmed he is seriously considering a move to the PNG Chiefs, revealing that contract talks with the expansion side have been ongoing for several weeks.

The Sydney Roosters utility also admitted he was impressed by what he saw during his recent visit to Port Moresby, saying the reality of Papua New Guinea was very different from the image often shown in the media.

It is believed the Chiefs have offered Watson a two-year contract beginning in 2028.

When questioned by ABC Sports Podcast host Andrew Moore about the reported deal, Watson confirmed the offer was genuine.

“Yeah,” Watson responded when asked if the Chiefs had tabled a two-year contract.

Watson recently travelled to Port Moresby to inspect the setup being prepared for the 19th NRL franchise and said the experience changed his perspective.

“We probably only saw the really good things,” Watson admitted.

“But everyone we spoke to, what’s portrayed in the media is a different sort of Port Moresby to the one we saw.”

Watson explained that one of the biggest areas he focused on during the visit was understanding what daily life in PNG would actually look like if he and his family decided to relocate.

“I think the big one for us was if we’re going to live over there,” he said.

“Even just things like groceries, produce and where that comes from – going and checking out those sorts of things.”

The 30-year-old said discussions about his future have now stretched back to the opening rounds of the NRL season, with negotiations continuing for almost three months.

“I’d like to get it wrapped up sooner rather than later,” Watson said.

“It’s dragged on since pretty much round one now. It’s been about 12 weeks of back and forth.”

Unlike previous contract negotiations earlier in his career, Watson said this process has been far more positive, with several clubs reportedly showing interest in securing his services.

“There’s actually a few clubs interested, which is a nice feeling,” he explained.

Despite the growing interest from rival teams, Watson said he wanted a decision finalised soon so he could focus on finishing the season strongly with the Roosters.

“I would like to get it wrapped up and then be able to concentrate on playing good footy for the Roosters,” he said.

Watson also confirmed that clubs were discussing both short-term and long-term contract possibilities with him, suggesting the Chiefs were not the only team chasing his signature.

“I think there’s scope for both,” he said. “There’s been conversations around both of those things.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Small surgeries make big impact
item.description: THE introduction of laparoscopic surgery at Port Moresby General Hospital (PMGH) marks a major advancement in surgical care in Papua New Guinea. Led by Dr Arnold Waine, the shift from traditional open surgery to minimally invasive techniques demonstrate how local expertise and innovation can improve healthcare outcomes.
item.content:encoded:
THE introduction of laparoscopic surgery at Port Moresby General Hospital (PMGH) marks a major advancement in surgical care in Papua New Guinea.

Led by Dr Arnold Waine, the shift from traditional open surgery to minimally invasive techniques demonstrate how local expertise and innovation can improve healthcare outcomes.

Laparoscopic surgery uses small 5-10 mm incisions, a camera, and specialised instruments to perform procedures with greater precision and less trauma to the body.

Compared with open surgery, patients experience less pain, lower infection risk, and shorter recovery times. Many patients can now be discharged within two to three days, significantly reducing pressure on hospital resources and lowering costs for families and the health system.

Equally important is the focus on training.

Waine and his team are teaching undergraduate and postgraduate students from the University of Papua New Guinea and other medical institutions to use laparoscopic equipment and techniques.

This ensures the technology is not limited to a small group of specialists but becomes part of PNG’s long-term surgical capacity.

The Government and hospital administration must now ensure reliable funding for equipment, maintenance, and training programs.

The Health Department and PNG Medical Board should also formalise laparoscopic standards within national surgical guidelines.

This initiative should not remain a one-off achievement at PMGH.

With proper support, laparoscopic surgery can expand to regional hospitals and provide safer, more efficient surgical care to more Papua New Guineans nationwide.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Delicate balance at Paris embassy
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea’s decision to open an embassy in Paris marks an important step as PNG and France celebrate 50 years of diplomatic relations. The move could strengthen cooperation in climate change, trade, security, environmental protection, and development.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea’s decision to open an embassy in Paris marks an important step as PNG and France celebrate 50 years of diplomatic relations.

The move could strengthen cooperation in climate change, trade, security, environmental protection, and development.

Supporters see opportunities for economic growth, tourism, education partnerships, and access to climate funding and scientific expertise.

Closer ties with France may also improve regional cooperation through its Pacific territories, including New Caledonia and French Polynesia.

However, concerns about sovereignty and foreign influence are valid.

The key issue is not whether France is a good or bad partner, but whether PNG can maintain a balanced and independent foreign policy that protects its national interests.

PNG must avoid becoming overly dependent on any single foreign power.

Australia has long been a major partner, and while that relationship has benefitted PNG, it also highlights the importance of maintaining strategic independence.

A balanced “Selective Engagement” approach would allow PNG to build partnerships with countries such as France, Australia, China, the United States, Japan, and Indonesia while protecting sovereignty and avoiding over-dependence.

Opening an embassy in Paris is neither automatically risky, nor automatically beneficial.

 Its success will depend on strong governance, transparent agreements, and leadership that ensures foreign policy always serves the long-term interests of PNG and its people.

Reginald Renagi Strategic leadership and governance advisor-advocate

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Merit over personality
item.description: PUBLIC discussion on Papua New Guinea’s political leadership, including on social media, reflects a healthy democratic process where citizens assess governance performance, development outcomes, and national direction. It is widely recognised that during the tenure of a former prime minister, significant investments were made in infrastructure, health, education, and district development programme
item.content:encoded:
PUBLIC discussion on Papua New Guinea’s political leadership, including on social media, reflects a healthy democratic process where citizens assess governance performance, development outcomes, and national direction.

It is widely recognised that during the tenure of a former prime minister, significant investments were made in infrastructure, health, education, and district development programmes.

These initiatives expanded service delivery and improved access in both rural and urban areas.

Such contributions should be acknowledged and assessed fairly as part of PNG’s development record.

At the same time, PNG’s democracy depends on renewal, accountability, and leadership succession.

No system is strengthened by permanent leadership, it is strengthened through open competition, strong institutions, and the emergence of capable new leaders. Leadership selection must prioritise merit, competence, integrity, and national vision.

The “best among equals” principle in Parliament remains important in ensuring leadership is chosen based on capability and service to the national interest rather than personality or political alignment.

Ultimately, PNG’s progress depends on strong institutions, sound governance, and equitable development.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pathway to reform medical licensing
item.description: THE absence of a standardised licensing process for overseas-trained medical graduates in Papua New Guinea creates uncertainty for applicants and inefficiency for the PNG Medical Board. Without published criteria, timelines, or assessment pathways, applicants are left guessing while the board handles cases manually, increasing delays and inconsistency.
item.content:encoded:
THE absence of a standardised licensing process for overseas-trained medical graduates in Papua New Guinea creates uncertainty for applicants and inefficiency for the PNG Medical Board.

Without published criteria, timelines, or assessment pathways, applicants are left guessing while the board handles cases manually, increasing delays and inconsistency.

A transparent, written process would protect both the public and applicants by clearly defining expectations.

PNG can learn from countries such as Australia and the United States, which use standardised pathways for internationally trained doctors.

The goal is not to copy these systems, but to adopt the principle of one published pathway, one set of requirements, and one clear timeline for all applicants.

A practical reform would be a tiered assessment system for graduates from recognised medical schools listed in the World Directory of Medical Schools.

This pathway should include digital credential verification, clinical competency assessment, and a supervised probationary period.

Every step should have a published timeframe and responsible officer.

Digitisation is essential to reducing backlogs.

An online portal for document uploads, application tracking, and status updates would improve transparency and reduce administrative burden.

The board should also publish service timelines and communicate delays clearly.

Standardisation does not lower standards, it applies them consistently.

With PNG facing a severe doctor shortage, qualified graduates cannot remain idle due to unclear processes.

Reforming the system within six months through consultation with stakeholders would ensure future graduates know exactly how to register and begin serving their communities.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific concerns about militarisation and NZ’s role in it
item.description: NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region. The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.
item.content:encoded:
NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region.

The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.

In recent months, the defence and police forces of Australia and New Zealand have been increasing cooperation with counterparts in Pacific countries, including Fiji, Tonga, Samoa and Vanuatu in efforts to combat transnational crime, especially the illicit drug trade. But as a number of Pacific Island countries weigh up signing major bilateral treaties or agreements with the likes of Australia, China and the United States (US), New Zealand has been steadily pushing Pacific regional defence cooperation on a number of fronts.

The communiqué from last October’s South Pacific Defence Ministers Meeting (SPDMM) in Chile is instructive.

The SPDMM – which involves New Zealand, Australia, Chile, Fiji, France, Papua New Guinea and Tonga – notes the leadership role New Zealand has taken on better coordinating regional defence architecture.

The member countries agreed to push for a defence advisor from their collective to be embedded in the secretariat of the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF), whose secretary-general Baron Waqa attended the Chile meeting and appeared to support closer integration.

While the advisor position is yet to be established, the SPDMM is surging ahead with a range of new regional defence initiatives, including developing the Pacific Response Group, under which defence personnel from Australia, Fiji, France, New Zealand and PNG work together to support coordinated humanitarian assistance and disaster relief responses across the Pacific.

A briefing from March’s joint meeting of the defence and foreign ministers of Australia and New Zealand, emphasised how they aim to promote “the sense of integration through Pacific defence forces’ and to ‘enhance the sense of Pacific forces meeting Pacific security needs”.

It also highlighted a keenness to “get more links between SPDMM and PIF so that these voices are heard directly by the region”.

“The briefings reveal New Zealand’s role in integrating and aligning Pacific defence forces alongside a considerable anxiety about regional social licence,” Pacific historian Marco de Jong said.

He said the language being used “speaks to a programme of influence and public relations, calibrated to downplay criticism that New Zealand is contributing to the militarisation of the Pacific”.

A representative with Development Alternatives with Women for a New Era, Maureen Penjueli, who is also a long time advocate for indigenous rights in the region, said there had been a lack of consultation with the wider Pacific Islands region about the new defence tack.

“We’ve seen so much occupation by those in the defence interests area. For example, the Australian National Security College takes a very primary seat at the Pacific Islands Forum on security. We’ve got competing interests, which is the Fusion Centre that’s in Vanuatu,” Penjueli said.

“When you add more and more players to this regional architecture that already has enough players on defence and security, it complicates the governance structure in a way.

“It does not go through the rigour of national consultations, consultations with civil society around some of these bigger significant shifts around defence and security.”

New Zealand is a long-standing contributor to Pacific regional initiatives, and its defence force is well valued in the region, especially in responding to disasters, humanitarian needs, transnational crime and maritime security threats, and also including in training support.

New Zealand’s defence minister Chris Penk, who replaced Judith Collins in the role since March’s 2+2 Ministerial Meeting with Australia, said that New Zealand always sought to adapt its work with Pacific partners to their context, culture and operational needs.

He told RNZ that in order to support closer cooperation between Pacific militaries, members were also looking at a SPDMM Status of Forces Agreement.

“This would provide a common legal framework for personnel to deploy into each other’s countries more easily, strengthening our collective ability to respond to maritime security challenges as well as humanitarian and disaster relief events.”

Asked if New Zealand is contributing to militarisation of the Pacific Islands, Penk said Pacific partners had warmly welcomed the country’s continued presence and partnership in the region

“The New Zealand defence force contributes to regional responses where it is agreed that defence force personnel and assets should be involved, including humanitarian assistance, maritime domain awareness, fisheries patrols, and search and rescue operations.

“As a Pacific military, we are proud to work alongside our Pacific partners to help respond to the challenges facing our region.”

Penjueli warned that militarisation of the region was escalating against the wishes of most Pacific Islands people.

Making things more complicated, she said, was the growing number of security treaties and agreements that Island countries were being drawn to.

She said they were no longer just about defence or security inter-operability, and often included development and economic dimensions, arrangements that “entangled” Pacific countries into wide ranging commitments beyond traditional military and security ties.

Penjueli worried that the interests of the Islands countries themselves were more than ever being buried under broader geopolitical jostling.

“We were told that this is to ready the region in an anticipation, to contain China, and we’re told that this is about the drug trade and the drug war that’s taking place.

“Yet for the Pacific, climate change or the climate crisis, remains our significant issue around security. So, I think the agendas are very different.”

At their last leaders summit, PIF countries signed up to the Blue Pacific Ocean of Peace Declaration, formally committing the region to peace, sovereignty, and climate justice.

However, Penjueli said being a true ocean of peace required demilitarisation and de-escalation – something which she suggested is not the direction that the defence-oriented governments of the region are heading in. – RNZ

item.title: Ex-security guard Zeming graduates with diploma
item.description: THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday. “In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had
item.content:encoded:
THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday.

“In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had graduated earlier (in 2022) and she used to challenge us to obtain qualifications (from tertiary institutions) as well.”

Zeming, 31, of Zankoa village in Tewae-Siassi district, Morobe used to score good marks in primary school but never became a top student or dux.

“I was never a dux. This is my first time being a dux. I thank God and my family for this.”

Zeming is the third eldest of five siblings – two brothers and three sisters. Their father Mao Zeming is self-employed while Mum Fredah died in 2018.

Zeming did not complete school because of financial issues. So he took on informal jobs to enroll in the diploma course at ITI.

“I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

His job as a security guard kindled his interest in management.

“I worked as a security and was happy to interact with people. I felt like I could manage their small complaints and issues. It got me interested in the management course.”

He faced challenges during the two years of study.

“The first two years were not easy. I tried my best to go to work and attend classes. And in the end, I achieved this.”

His family motivated him to get his diploma.

“We are a close-knit family. Every time my brother would counsel me. He reminded me of our humble beginnings and not to take education for granted. It was their motivation that kept me going.”

His father’s strict rules on time guided his study routine.

“ I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

“Focus is one thing that my father talks about every time, reminding us to be early before schedule and all that. He is a very strict man, very strict on time.

“Some things we don’t know about, he encourages us to ask people for help, to be curious. So yes, it was my father’s strictness that had shaped my study routine.”

“I always make sure that I did my homework and my studies and that things are in order.

“The good thing is that, I have a good family. They are very understanding people. Once in a while they would ask me for help. But mostly, they respect me; they played their part and mine as well.”

“My brother is always my role model. He used to remind us that success is there, all you need to do to achieve it is to remain focused.

“He does not have a big job but he ensures that we all are taken care of. He took on the family responsibility and I have respect for him.”

Zeming is happy to have accomplished his goal.

“I am really happy. Seeing (cousin sister) Luddy also graduating today makes me really happy. We helped each other at school. We also help other Morobe students as well and in the end, we made it.”

Zeming dedicated the dux award to his late mother and his diploma to his father.

“I dedicated these achievements to my family, those who had helped me with my school fees and my upbringing. And this dux award is dedicated to my late mum. Dad gets the diploma.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”
